title: Task-Centric Acceleration of Small-Language Models
abstract: Small language models (SLMs) have emerged as efficient alternatives to large language mod- els for task-specific applications. However, they are often employed in high-volume, low- latency settings, where efficiency is crucial. We propose TASC, Task-Adaptive Sequence Com- pression, a framework for SLM acceleration comprising two use-cases: When performing SLM fine-tuning, we propose TASC-ft, which iteratively enriches the tokenizer vocabulary with high-frequency output n-grams and then fine-tunes the model to utilize the expanded vo- cabulary. Next, we propose an inference-time method, termed TASC-spec. TASC-spec is a lightweight, training-free speculative decoding method that constructs an n-gram draft model from the task’s output corpus, mixing task and context n-gram information. TASC-spec avoids any additional training, while avoiding draft-target vocabulary alignment constraints. We demonstrate the effectiveness of both meth- ods across multiple low output-variability gen- eration tasks. Our methods show consistent im- provements in inference efficiency while main- taining task performance. 1 Introduction Small language models (SLMs) are lightweight fast models that recently rose as a viable alternative to large language models (LLMs) (Belcak et al., 2025; Subramanian et al., 2025). SLMs are often distilled versions of LLMs that pose specific capabilities, with the intention to operate as a part of a multi- agent system (Lee et al., 2025; Sharma and Mehta, 2025) or in highly memory-bound high-latency set- tings, such as on-device deployment (Kementched- jhieva and Chalkidis, 2023; Kandala et al., 2024; Lu et al., 2025). SLMs, while lighter than their larger counterparts, have demonstrated compara- ble performance when fine-tuned (FT) on specific tasks, from text classification (Luo et al., 2023a; *Work was done during an internship at Amazon. Sun et al., 2023a; Sakai and Lam, 2025) and sum- marization (Xu et al., 2025; Bailly et al., 2025), to question-answering (QA) (Pappas et al., 2022; Kweon et al., 2024) and code generation (Luo et al., 2023b; Pinnaparaju et al., 2024). We argue that tasks naturally suited for SLM FT also often exhibit lower output variability com- pared to open-ended multi-purpose text generation. This manifests in a decrease of the n-gram empir- ical entropy in the dataset of expected task out- puts. That is, expected output texts tend to contain more n-grams that repeat across different samples. These n-grams often represent common phrases and terms in the task’s expected outputs. We ana- lyze this phenomenon through the lenses of typi- cality (Cover, 1999, Chapter 2). To accelerate LLMs, most prior work focus on either decreasing memory requirements or accel- erating input processing, with the purpose of han- dling longer contexts. Popular methods in these direction are model quantization (Lang et al., 2024; Lin et al., 2024), KV caching (Pope et al., 2023; Li et al., 2024a) and attention optimization (Dao et al., 2022; Kwon et al., 2023). On the contrary, in this work we follow the low output variability, com- mon in FT SLMs, and focus on the model’s outputs. Specifically, we view efficiency in terms of decreas- ing the total number of model auto-regressive steps required to generate a response from a given task input, which is a known bottleneck in autoregres- sive generation (OpenAI, 2025). To that end, our framework comprising two solutions, which we term Task-Adaptive Sequence Compression (TASC). Each solution targets a different operational SLM use-case. Figure 1 outlines TASC’s pipeline. First, when task-specific fine-tuning of the SLM is performed, we propose TASC-ft, a tokenizer augmentation procedure that explicitly leverages the task-induced concentration of output n-grams. Given a train corpus of expected task outputs, we identify high-frequency n-grams whose merge into 1 arXiv:2602.24174v1 [cs.CL] 27 Feb 2026

paragraphs.text: single Tesla V-100 GPU (32GB). Hyperparameter
values are based on preliminary evaluations and
ablations. Full details are given in Appendix B.

(a) EUR-LEX (massive classification)
(b) Asclepius (medical QA)
Figure 3: TASC-ft: Task performance metric vs. average runtime vs. Number of Added tokens. Each dataset has a
different metric according to its task: F1 for EUR-LEX, Bert-score for Asclepius and join accurcay for MASSIVE.
TASC-ft: Tokenizer Augmentation
We test the effect of TASC-ft on the resulting fine-
tuned model. We measure the effect of the added
tokens (and consequently, trainable parameters) on
the trained model’s performance. Our baseline is
the same base model, going through similar train-
ing, but without the newly introduced n-gram to-
kens. In all experiments we consider a Qwen2.5-
0.5B base model, employ QLoRa optimization
(Dettmers et al., 2023) and train for 3 epochs. Due
to space constraints, additional performance met-
rics and ablations are reported in Appendix C.
Datasets. We consider three tasks: (i) massive
text classification for which we use the EUR-LEX
dataset (Chalkidis et al., 2019). It contains 19.6K
policy texts, each labeled with up to 26 labels out of
a total of 4K possible labels, (ii) Medical QA, for
which we consider the Asclepius dataset (Kweon
et al., 2024). We focus on the QA category that
consists of 11K QA pairs, (iii) Intent classification
& slot filling (ICSF), for which we consider the
MASSIVE dataset (FitzGerald et al., 2023) that
contains of 16.5K english utterances with intent
and slot fills labels. The dataset contains 50 distinct
intent categories and 60 slot fill categories.
Metrics. For each dataset, we measure the aver-
age time to generate a response and calculate the
response’s quality on a task-specific evaluation met-
ric. For EUR-LEX we consider the F1 score, and
for Asclepius we consider both the BERT score
(Zhang et al., 2019) and a LLM-judge score, based
on the evaluation prompt from (Kweon et al., 2024)
using Claude4.5-Sonnet as the judge. For ICSF we
consider both intent accuracy, slot fill F1 score and
joint accuracy. To follow a real-world use case, we
consider batched generation with b = 8.
Results. We examine the effect of the number of
tokens added, comparing to the same base model
undergoing comparable training without newly in-
troduced n-gram tokens. As shown in Figure 3,
training with the extended tokenizer preserves task
performance while achieving faster average gen-
eration time on the test set. Table 2 measures a
judge-LLM evaluations of the Asclepius outputs,
showing negligible impact on the output quality.
Our experiments consider a token budget of up
to 1000 n-grams. While increasing the number of
added tokens consistently reduces inference-time
generation latency, the marginal gains diminish as
the budget grows, indicating a trade-off between
acceleration and the training parameters overhead.
In practice, the effective cutoff improvement in our
experiments is no larger than 500 tokens, which is
significantly smaller than the token budgets typi-
cally used in domain-base tokenization adaptations
(Nakash et al., 2025). We leave a principled analy-
sis of the optimal TASC-ft budget for future work.
Predicting Runtime Reduction. Fine-tuning is
computationally expensive, motivating the need
for a lightweight proxy to assess a model’s poten-
tial benefit from TASC-ft. We examine whether a
dataset-level statistic can serve as a reliable predic-
tor of inference runtime. Specifically, we consider
the 2-Rényi entropy (H2) of the token distribution,
previously linked to downstream performance. Our
Figure 4: Acceptance rate vs. speculated token position.
experiments comprise 124 runs across 8 configura-
tions, where runs within each configuration differ
only in the token budget. We consider two com-
plementary criteria: (i) Kendall’s τ, computed sep-
arately for each configuration, yields τ < −0.9
(p < 0.005) in all cases, indicating a strong and
consistent inverse correlation. We measure the
rank correlation using Kendall’s τ, all yielding
τ < −0.9 with p-value < 0.005. This indicates a
near-perfect inverse agreement. (ii) Following (Pe-
saran and Timmermann, 1992), we asses how often
a change in H2 correctly predicts the direction of
change in runtime. We observed a 92% success
rate, confirming that H2 is a highly reliable indi-
cator for predicting runtime reduction. Additional
details are provided in Appendix E.
TASC-spec: Inference-Time Acceleration
We compare our results with the n-grammy’s
method (Stewart et al., 2024), which proposes a
bigram-based drafter that is obtained by taking the
conditional LLM distribution for each element in
the tokenizer’s vocabulary. To maintain compara-
bility we consider task fine-tuned LLMs, to ensure
that its conditional distribution is adapted to the
task as hand. We consider a beam-size of b = 1
and apply TASC-spec with pmin = 5 and λ = 0.75.
Datasets and Models We consider two medi-
cal QA tasks, given by the Asclepius (Kweon
et al., 2024) and MedQuaD (Ben Abacha and
Demner-Fushman, 2019) datasets, and EUR-LEX.
Asclepius focuses on reading comprehension of
medical texts, whereas MedQuaD does not contain
an additional context for each question.
Speedup over target model. We evaluate perfor-
mance by calculating the average speedup over the
target model across 50 test samples per genera-
tion task. We report the performance of the corpus
Table 3: Target speedup results in several datasets.
TASC-spec is employed with λ = 0.75.
mixing with the context-based n-gram drafter. As
seen in Table 3, the corpus n-gram achieves a better
target speedup over the n-grammy’s drafter. This
advantage is maintained after mixing with pprompt,
emphasizing the power of global corpus informa-
tion for speculative decoding.
Position-wise Acceptance. Due to the negligible
cost of obtaining a prediction from n-gram mod-
els we take long prediction horizons, which are
then verified in-parallel by the target model, with-
out suffering a significant latency overhead. We
provide a finer granularity for acceptance by an-
alyzing the acceptance rate for each position in
TASC-spec’s continuations separately. As seen in
Figure 4, the acceptance rate drops as the posi-
tion index increases. This is due to the expected
distribution shift of the n-gram model. Although
the n-gram model is a highly simplified language
model, we obtain relatively high acceptance rates in
initial positions, with first position acceptance rate
α = 0.57 in Asclepius and α = 0.39 in MedQuaD.
This paper presented TASC, an SLM acceleration
framework that exploits the n-gram typical set in
low output variability tasks. The proposed meth-
ods consider either fine-tune setting via tokenizer
augmentation or inference-time acceleration using
n-gram draft models.
Moreover, we study the
power of the token distribution’s Rényi entropy
as a predictive measure of potential runtime ac-
celeration. The proposed methods substantially
reduce the number of decoding steps while preserv-
ing output quality. For future work we consider a
comprehensive analysis of the optimal token bud-
get for TASC-ft following the diminishing returns
in terms of newly added tokens.
The proposed methodologies suffer from several
limitations. First, we assume that the considered
task has an available representative dataset, which
we can use for typicality analysis. We implicitly
assume that the training dataset we utilize captures
the underlying distribution in the test set. The suc-
cess of our method is limited to generation tasks
that exhibit the low output variability demonstrated
The tokenizer augmentation procedure hinges
on the assumption that we have access to the model
weights and that we have the computational capa-
bilities to fine-tune that model. Furthermore, by
augmenting the model’s tokenizer, we may harm its
generalist capabilities and decrease its performance
in other tasks. However, we anticipate this effect
to be less significant than both adding and remov-
ing tokens from the vocabulary, as often done in
domain-based methods (as, e.g., in (Nakash et al.,

Additional Information on Byte-Pair
BPE (Gage, 1994) is a simple frequency-based al-
gorithm that constructs a subword vocabulary in
a data-driven manner. Given a training corpus C,
BPE learns both a vocabulary V and an ordered list
of merge operations.
The algorithm starts from a base vocabulary
V0, typically consisting of individual characters or
bytes. Each element of the corpus is initially repre-
sented as a sequence of symbols from V0. At each
iteration, BPE identifies the most frequent token
bigram in the corpus and merges it into a new to-
ken, which is added to the vocabulary. This merge
operation replaces all occurrences of the selected
pair with the newly created single token.
The merging procedure iteratively repeats for
M iterations, resulting in a final vocabulary V =
M}, along with an ordered merge
table that records the sequence in which merges
were applied. At inference time, new text is tok-
enized by greedily applying these learned merges
in order, producing a sequence of tokens drawn
from V. Following the HuggingFace implementa-
tion of tokenizer vocabulary augmentation, when
adding new tokens to an existing vocabulary, their
corresponding merge operations are not added to
the merge table. They are stored in a different data
structure. Upon encoding, the set of new tokens
is queried for a possible continuation prior to the
base tokenizer’s merge table.
Additional Implementation Details
LLM Judge Information
The judge we use to generate the results in Table 2
is Claude-4.5 Sonnet. We deploy the LLM evalua-
tion pipeline through Amazon Bedrock. The scores
are calculated out of 1260 output samples. The
evaluation prompt is given Figure 9. It is based
the judge evaluation prompt from (Kweon et al.,
2024). To avoid position bias in the response posi-
tion in the evaluation prompt, we randomly select
the order of LLM response in each sample.
Additional Training Information
Applying TASC-ft requires fine-tuning the model
at hand with the updated token vocabulary. We
fine-tuned the model using Quantized Low-Rank
Adaptation (QLoRA) (Dettmers et al., 2023) with
4-bit quantization via the BitsAndBytes library,
employing the NF4 quantization type with double
quantization enabled and bfloat16 precision for
compute. We consider LoRa rank r = 16 and
α = 16 (i.e., a scaling factor of 1.0), and dropout
rate of 0.2. The LoRA adapters were applied to the
attention layers as well as the feed-forward layers.
Additionally, the embedding layer and language
modeling head were retrained to accommodate the
extended vocabulary with new task-specific tokens.
Training was conducted with an initial learning rate
of 2 × 10−4 using a linear learning rate scheduler
and the AdamW optimizer (Loshchilov and Hutter,
2017). We used a per-device batch size of 8 and an
effective batch size of 64.
Additional Datasets and Models
The datasets considered in Table 1 are described
in Table 4. In our experiments, we consider the
1. Asclepius (Kweon et al., 2024), a synthetic
dataset of discharge summaries and instruc-
tions, aimed toward domain-based reading
comprehension. It contains 8 generation tasks.
In this work we consider the QA task. It com-
prises 11K samples of (discharge summary,
instruction) pairs and an expected output.
Fushman, 2019), an open-ended QA dataset,
where the questions do no contain any
This dataset tests the
model’s knowledge rather than its retrieval
and context comprehension capabilities. The
dataset contains 10K samples.
3. EUR-LEX (Chalkidis et al., 2019), a massive
multi-label multi-class classification dataset
of policy texts. Each sample contains a a col-
lection of EU policy recitals and a sequence of
labels. To transform this task into a text gen-
eration task, we map each label to its textual
name, given by a provided label-name dictio-
nary. To maintain a constant order of labels,
we order each label sequence numerically ac-
cording to label ID prior to its decoding into a
sequence of label names. The dataset contains
57K samples, each with up to 26 labels out
of a set of 4K labels.
4. MASSIVE (FitzGerald et al., 2023) an intent-
classification & slot filling dataset, curated

Additional Experiment Results and
TASC-ft: Additional Evaluation Metrics
Additional Metrics in MASSIVE Dataset: We
complement TASC-ft’s results on the ICSF task
from Section 6 with additional metrics. Specif-
ically, we measure the intent accuracy and slot
prediction F1 score, to present a finer granular-
ity of the fine-tuned model’s behavior on the test
set. For completeness, we present these metrics
alongside the metrics reported in Section 6. As
seen from Table 5, both intent accuracy and slot F1
scores present a slight improvement when introduc-
ing new n-gram tokens. This trend is in line with
the joint accuracy, which is a coarser and stricter
measure of performance.
Precision-Recall: We present the fine-tuned
model’s precision-recall trade off. As the main pa-
rameter of our framework is the number of added
tokens, we compare the trade-of on models trained
with different numbers of added tokens. We recall
that the latency improvement was already estab-
lished and studied in Section 6. Figure 5 presents
the precision-recall trade-off for two values of num-
ber of training epochs. As seen from Figure 5,
introducing new tokens improves precision-recall
trade-off, with the best performance at M = 250
new tokens. These results are aligned with Table 5,
which shows that task performance improvement
after introducing new tokens, both in terms of in-
tent and slot fill prediction. To that end, while
existing literature often discusses a hard trade-off
between runtime acceleration and output quality,
we observe that this is not necessarily the case in
low output variability generation with SLMs.
Next, we consider the EUR-LEX dataset. We
observe the precision-recall trade-off for several
values of maxN, the maximal merge size in the
TASC-ft algorithm (Algorithm 1). As seen in Fig-
ure 6, the number of added tokens has a relatively
negligible on the trade-off. However, we note a uni-
form increase in precision across all experiments.

better speedup of the baseline. However, it requires

TASC-spec: Ablation on Mixing
We test the effect of the mixing parameter λ value
in TASC-spec. To do that, we curate a dedicated
validation set of 150 samples from each dataset. As
seen in Figure 7, For λ ∈[0.5, 0.9], we result in a
mixture distribution that improves upon both the
corpus n-gram and the prompt information. We
note that highest speedup is attained within the
interval λ ∈[0.5, 0.75], which implies that the un-
derlying distributions are relatively spiky. That is,
each distribution proposes a next token with rela-
tively high probability in distinct cases, resulting in
more accepted token overall. We note that unlike n-
grammys (Stewart et al., 2024), the n-gram distribu-
tions are independent of the underlying model, and
that potential misalignment with the model might
decrease performance, while the n-gram model
properly predict the "task ground truth" next token.
Uniform Information Density -
We provide empirical evidence that the effects in-
duced by TASC-ft are consistent with the uniform
information distribution (UID) hypothesis. UID
predicts that efficient representations distribute in-
formation more evenly across tokens, reducing con-
centration of probability mass and stabilizing per-
token surprisal along sequences. By collapsing
frequent task-specific n-grams into atomic sym-
bols, TASC-ft increases the size of the token larger
typical set. As a result, the empirical output token
distribution becomes less peaked and exhibits a
heavier tail. This effect is visualized in Figure 8,
where applying TASC-ft leads to a noticeably flat-
ter distribution compared to the baseline tokenizer.
We further quantify this uniformization by mea-
suring the entropy of the output token distribution
before and after applying TASC-ft.
normalized entropy, obtained by dividing the em-
pirical entropy by the maximal possible entropy,
log |V|. As shown in Table 9, TASC-ft consistently
increases token entropy across settings, indicating
Predicting Model Speedup in TASC-ft
preprocessing-time statistic such as the Rényi en-
tropy of order 2 (H2) can serve as a reliable proxy
for predicting runtime reduction after fine-tuning.
Following (Zouhar et al., 2023), Rényi entropy of
the token distribution was shown to be correlated
with model performance. Furthermore, we con-
sider entropic measures are a proxy for the size of
the typical set due to (Cover, 1999) (See Section 3).

train set), fine-tune the model with the augmented
tokenizer and record the average runtime on the

(M, H2(M), runtime(M)) for each set of hyper-
parameter values. Our goal is to analyze the cor-
relation of predictive power of H2 for fine-tuned
model runtime reduction.
Ordering Consistency via Rank
To assess the predictive power of H2, we com-
pute Kendall’s rank correlation coefficient τ be-
tween H2(M) and runtime(M) for each sequence.
Kendall’s τ measures how consistently two quanti-
ties induce the same ordering over a set of items. A
higher value of τ indicates that pairs of items that
are ranked higher by one metric are also more likely
to be ranked higher by the other, reflecting stronger
agreement in their relative ordering. Conversely,
values of τ close to zero indicate little systematic
agreement beyond chance, while negative values
indicate that the two metrics tend to induce oppos-
ing orderings. We compute τ separately for each
sequence and report aggregate statistics across se-
quences, thereby assessing robustness to dataset
and hyperparameter variation. Across all 124 sim-
ulations, divided into 8 settings we observe a mean
Kendall’s τ of −0.964, indicating strong and con-
sistent ordering agreement between H2(M) and
Directional Predictive Reliability
While rank correlation captures global ordering
agreement, it does not directly address the op-
erational question of interest:
the H2(M) reliably lead to an improvement in
runtime(M)? (Pesaran and Timmermann, 1992)
To answer this, we analyze parameter intervals
updates each sequence. For consecutive values
Mi, Mi+1, we compute
∆R = runtime(Mi+1) −runtime(Mi).
Conditioning on updates where the Rényi en-
tropy decreases (∆(H2) > 0), we measure the
conditional success rate
i.e., the probability that runtime decreases despite
given an increase in the Rényi entropy. Its comple-
ment is defined as a directional success rate. Based
on the data collected from 124 simulations, the
directional success rate is 92%, implying that in-
crease in H2 are followed by runtime reductions in
the vast majority of cases.

3. Satisfactory (3 points): The model’s response does not contain any incorrect

4. Excellent (4 points): The model’s response contains all necessary information.
If the instruction was unanswerable, the model correctly acknowledged this.
